Um, so the RCA is the only exclusively post-graduate art and design university in the world. It was founded all the way back in 1837 as the government school of design and then in the 1850s we became the National Art Training School based at South Kensington where we still are. Um, we grew up as part of the new cultural complex that followed on from the 1851 Great Exhibition. Uh and that complex also included the Victoria and Albert Museum and we still have a lot of institutional links with them. Uh we finally became the Royal College of Art in 1896, but we've always been much more than a traditional art college and we've always tried to balance art and design. Um lot of uh involvement in cutting edge technology. Um a lot of interest in fostering interdisciplinarity in the creative fields. So, we're often known for our star painting alumni, uh, such as David Hawkchney and Bridget Riley, but there have been many achievements across all the design fields as well. Um, textile designers of the 50s and 60s like Althia Mcnish and Xandra Rhodess, industrial designers James Dyson and Thomas Heatherwick, and fashion designers including Philip Tracy, Erdem and Cla Keller. So today we have over 2,000 students following over 20 different programs at M's level or above. So what exactly is the color reference library and how did it end up at the RCA? Well, it's pretty much as its fairly prosaic name describes, it is a special collection of material, largely books, that engages fundamentally with the subject of color uh in all its forms and all its disciplines. It's not just a collection, it's a kind of physical manifestation of a huge body of knowledge. It spans seven centuries and it covers every possible aspect and format of its subject from the history of pigments to color psychology. It includes all the key figures that you'd expect from Isaac Newton to Yoseph Alves and it also takes in a lot of ephemeral and proprietary color charts and even a few archives. It's now probably the largest public collection of books on color in the world depending on how you count. So the earliest item that you can see here on the left is a 14th century manuscript analyzing the colors of the rainbow and their different personalities as inspired by the writings of Aristotle. The collection continues right up to the present day with lots of works from the 20th and 21st centuries. And on the right is a cover you might recognize, Chromophobia by David Bachelor. uh slim book uh about western anxiety around bright color which I'm sure um I'm sure you many of you know it uh it's been hugely influential since it was first published in 2000 but I mention it because it was partly researched at the color reference library when David was an RCA tutor on our uh curating contemporary art course. Now, the collection's origins can be found somewhere in this photo from around 1941, which I promise is the only black and white slide I have for you today. It's an image of the RCA students uh in the Second World War after they'd been evacuated to the rural Lake District in the northwest of England from our London premises after all the windows of our building got blown out in the Blitz. So the RCA essentially decamped to the lake district and took over two local hotels in Amblesside and teaching continued unbroken. So here are the students sketching on the slopes and having a very bohemian and bucolic war. There's obviously no enemy planes in the skies overhead in this particular picture. Um, one of these students was a young man named Donald Pavey who was studying painting at the RCA. And he went on to have a lifelong obsession with all kinds of esoteric subjects including alchemy, um, comparative religion, the psychology of games, all sorts of interesting stuff. But perhaps his biggest enthusiasm was reserved for color. And the subject obsessed him for decades, as it has gripped so many others throughout history. Uh Don ended up collecting a number of rare books on the subject uh over the years and he became an art tutor at Kingston School of Art in Southwest London and he always planned to use his collection to write a definitive history of color color systems but his own book never materialized. The sheer scale of the task defeated him. I can certainly relate to that. uh and he ended up selling the collection in the early 1970s to a colleague at Kingston named Stuart Durant. Stuart Durant was uh perhaps not coincidentally another RCA alumnus. Um they get everywhere I find. So here is the lovely Stuart who died in 2020 and this guardian obituary refers to the color reference library as one of his legacies. Now Stuart had lots of connections to the rare book trade and he substantially enhanced the collection through his um multiple multiple contacts. Uh and he was sort of poised ready to pounce whenever he knew an important work was going to come up at auction. And he also tracked down works by obscure and hidden figures who had published on the subject including Mary Gartside, Carrie Van Beamer and Maxwell Armfield. Some of whom have now been rediscovered recently rediscovered long after first publication. So Stuart had a good eye and he also played a long game. with the help of his friends in the antiquarian book trade, he eventually built up a really impressive collection um that really aspired to be essentially the national collection on color. This is part of the collection in situ today and the reason it ended up at the RCA is because of the college librarian of the 1970s, an erodite figure named Hance Bril, who is the third and final character in our origin story. So Hance mounted a successful campaign to buy the collection for the college when he heard it was going to be um sold abroad. Stuart was planning to sell it to a wealthy private collector. And the RCA thought quite rightly that it would be an excellent interdisciplinary resource for the students. One that could give RCA students a sure grounding in color theory while providing authoritative solutions to problems with color in design and inspiring new creations directly in the studio which it has always done and it still continues to do. So, it arrived at the college in the late 1970s uh containing about 700 titles. Um, but as I think Nick mentioned, that figure is now closer to 2,000. We still actively collect new books and we fill in gaps when we can get old books that are missing. It's not a closed collection in any way. And all of the big beasts, all of the titans of color theory are represented from the 11th century Arab polymath alen to the bow house figures of it and Alz. So the collection tries to accommodate the extraordinary reach and capacity of the subject of color and to examine it through every possible lens. There are books devoted to the color ranges of particular things from mushrooms to national flags to human skin tones. And there are books about the history and symbolism of individual colors and their application to different fields from architecture to textiles. There are also books about color perception, including optical illusions and after images. There are lots of books that are just about blue or just about red, but there are not very many that are just about purple or orange. But the subject is incredibly difficult to break into bite-sized chunks or to fit into a clear narrative or a classification scheme. I host a lot of group visits from people who come for an library and I usually pitch it as a wider introduction to the subject of color as a phenomenon as illustrated through some of the key texts we hold and I often break it into five broad sections to consider in turn how color has be historically been explained, organized, perceived, felt and imagined. And this means we get to cover everything from Newton's original color circle to Munel's 3D model of color to Lucia's color test. We'll come to these later. Uh and to balance objective and subjective approaches wherever possible. Um it is uh perfectly possible to be just entirely forensic and analytical about color. But this approach I find doesn't really gel with art students. Uh hence the need for that final section color imagined. Uh unfortunately, one of the things that I love about the collection is its open-mindedness. The scientific legitimacy of the titles uh hasn't always been as important as their approach to the subject. So while we have plenty of books uh that are about the physics and chemistry of color, we also have quite a bit of esoterica in the form of rather weird books about color healing and auras and the transcendental qualities of color-based experiences. So this is an area that spoke to both Don Pavey and Stuart Durant. They both shared an interest in mysticism. So it's not surprising there's plenty of room for it on the shelves. So, for example, while Stuart was putting the collection together in the 1970s, he acquired the unpublished color notebooks of an early 20th century artist called Maxwell Armfield, who had recently died. Armfield fell under the spell of the occult movements of Edwardian England, and he became obsessed with the idea that color might be a layer between our world and some other higher plane. uh he thought it was a secret code to be cracked and that if the spectrum could be mapped onto other concepts such as musical scales uh or the tarot deck or the signs of the zodiac things like that um it might reveal itself as one of the supernatural building blocks of the universe. Uh as you can see all approaches to the subject are valid in CRL. We don't judge and those ideas aren't a million miles away from Newton's anyway. Uh so these are some um examples from his published and unpublished work. His color Sophira diagram uh as a as an original drawing um on the left as part of his um archives that we that we hold and then how it appeared in print in one of his books on the right. Um so so the mystical or pseudoscientific dimensions of color recur across the collection and they sometimes pose challenges. For example, we have an interesting mid-century book on the healing power of colored rays by a certain Dr. Hilton. And it's notable not just because it was published postumously, but because it was actually written by the author after he died, dictated from beyond the grave through a spirit medium called Irene. It was a challenge for uh my colleague in the library who had to catalog it and had to decide whether to record a ghost or a fantasist as the principal author of the work. So how is the collection used? Well, uh books beget books and it is uh in frequent use by researchers working on publications about color, its history, cultural significance and application such as these here. It's also used by cultural historians, curators, and conservators to research available paints and standards in particular periods in history to help reverse engineer and better understand the works in their care, but is principally used uh as intended as a practical resource by artists and designers, especially RCA students who are conveniently already on site uh to support and inspire hundreds of new studio-based artworks and products and projects. So, this is a small selection of um RCA student projects that have been informed by contact with our books. Color itself never goes out of fashion. So, the library is both timeless and topical, and it's recently informed investigations into various issues of the day, including color and gender, uh, color and ethnicity, color naming, traditional recipes for natural dyes, and color and neurodeiversity. Our open-minded collecting policy sometimes makes it a tricky collection to organize and to market to the research community. And there are a lot of tensions in the collection, a lot of um arguments between the books in a way uh between art and science, between subjectivity and objectivity, and between the natural and the synthetic. No two pathways through the resource are alike. There are as many approaches and research questions as there are users. And this is a strength uh in a post-graduate art college where people often learn in unconventional ways. It's possible to have a rewarding session in the color reference library without actually reading a single word. Some visitors literally just look at the pictures or at least immerse themselves in the abundance of color palettes and samples on offer. But equally, it's possible to research your subject without encountering a single color plate. There's no color in any of the diagrams in Newton's optics, for instance. I recently made this guide to put up beside the titles in the store to help users navigate the shelves. Um, any librarians in in attendance this evening might recognize the famous Dewey decimal system that you still find in many public and some academic libraries today. And this is how the color reference library is uh organized. So the dewy decimal system is an American system that organizes all the world's knowledge into 10 main subject categories including religion, technology, science, and the arts with increasingly detailed subdivisions beneath each one. And I'm including this slide because it shows the astonishing granularity of the subject, how the subject of color can be broken down. um did a quick count and there are about a hundred different subjects identified here under the color umbrella from fortunetelling to camouflage to ink to city planning. You'll see that all 10 sections have at least a few color related topics under them. It's such an interdicciplinary subject because as we all know color is part of the fabric of life both of day-to-day human experience and our intentional selfexpression. It's something we receive and something we transmit, which makes the RCA an ideal home for it where our students could be studying anything from painting to fashion to healthcare design. So, smashing up the subject into a hundred fragments like this is both a help and a hindrance. Is often very difficult for catalogers to know exactly where to slot a particular title. And even among all these categories, there are multiple crossovers. Uh he was brought up during the French Revolution. Um he then undertook pioneering work on fats, candles. Uh he discovered and named cholesterol. But we know him for his contribution to color theory from when he worked at the prestigious Goblin Tapestry firm in the 1820s and was brought in to oversee their their dying processes. and he walked into the middle of a massive dispute between the weavers and the dyers of the firm. Um the weavers were complaining to the dyers that the quality of the dy just wasn't good enough. The colors seemed to be fading. The black colors were turning orangey orangey red. It was a potential PR disaster. So, Chroll investigated, did all the tests you would expect on the the color fastness of the dyes and discovered that the black was not actually fading, but its appearance was changing depending on the colors that surrounded it. Um, and that this was a kind of um permanent state of color, that it was endlessly fluid, and it would appear different according to our perception depending on whatever um context it appeared in. um and that any color would start to look a bit like the complimentary color of the color that it was next to. And what was so amazing was that was the the work that went into this that he worked it up into a scientific law. He saw that it was a universal phenomenon that it was a weird sort of unpredictability of color. But once you understood that it was an incredibly predictable um effect and that this was something that that people could work with. So he worked it up into this scientific law that he called simultaneous contrast. um and summarized it as by saying where two colors appear side by side, they will appear as dissimilar as as possible. Um so uh so he worked it up into this incredible um book from from 1839 that was absolutely packed full of advice um for different people working in different industries about how they could um maximize the effects of this weird behavior of color. um how you know um ctographers um museum curators, wallpaper designers if they knew that color behaved in this weird way they could you it became a sort of powerful tool that they could exploit. So for ctographers there was advice about sort of making um the text as as legible as possible on top of colored land masses. Chival identified that color contrast was at its most intense whenever you paired colors that are on opposite sides of the color wheel. Blue and orange, red and green, purple and yellow. So with blue and orange for instance because each color starts to take on a tinge of its um it's complimentary uh you get a bluer blue and you get a more orangey orange. So my next uh selection uh is about how colors are described, but it's also a good example of how color can be organized. It's often said that most people can only really identify 11 different broad color categories while they can perceive over a million different varieties. So clearly there's quite a big gap between language and experience of color. So this is a book called dictionary of color from 1930. Um it's an American book. It was compiled by two American color consultants Alois Mets and Morris Ray Paul. And it was an exhaustive attempt to standardize the color standards to try to unite 7,000 over 7,000 I think color samples and link them to their popular names in different paint charts and um other commercial systems that were available. Uh so it would this would be a definitive book that would show you exactly what royal blue was supposed to look like let's say or fuchsia. um color was booming in products uh and on the page at this point. So there was an a pressing need to establish a kind of common common language, a common vocabulary for all the infinite varieties that were spinning off and appearing from all directions. Different bodies would state different claims to color names and ranges and varieties. So there was the American Textile Color Card Association for example that had been busy publishing an annual physical sample of colors in fashion every year um from the 1910s onwards I think. So Mets and Paul spent years gathering this information and they compiled it by pulling together samples from all the known systems they could find. They gathered all the previous books, all the commercial samples and they cut everything up and they glued examples onto index cards. so that comparable colors could be viewed side by side and wherever there was a discrepancy if they had two slightly different versions of I don't know royal blue or whatever they'd average it out and find something in the middle. So the the layout of the book is like this and you have these color plates with all these varieties on the right hand side and then the terminology on the left. So you find your color sample and then you look at the coordinates, the corresponding coordinates on the left and it tells you what it what it should be called or what it has been called. And on this page, we've got names like baby pink and and scarlet. But the typography is important because if the title appears in all caps, then that means that's a color term that is in use at the point of the book being compiled. If it's just in title case, then that means it's a well-known name, but it's not in current use. And if it's in italics, it means it is just about still known, but effectively obsolete. So on this page, for instance, we've got um uh one square that has Fiesta and Crushed Strawberry in the same uh square. That's um J10 uh sorry, I 10. Um there's a sense that this is just you know um sticking a pin in in a in a fast changing industry and that there's a kind of futility in the whole endeavor kind of built-in obsolescence to this book because the the authors admit that this is going to be superseded in a few years time. So despite the enormity of the effort is just a snapshot of of color names in in particular in a particular moment. Um the authors I think they were they were acutely aware of their uh own obsolescence and and they even call it a dictionary of color not the dictionary of color. Um it's it's a it's a work in progress. Um but like a lot of color books uh like the chio it sort of falls into two parts. There's the text section and there's the colorbased section. Um, so you get you have all of the color plates and then you have this big chunk of descriptive text and a a a kind of history and biography of each color name at the back. And this glossery section is absolutely fascinating. um full of kind of little nuggets that I hadn't really realized until I studied it in great detail, such as the fact that um historically the difference in spelling um for gray, gray with an E and gray with an A um is because gray with an A was supposed to represent pure black and white, whereas gray with an E was supposed to represent that sort of gray with a tinge of another color added. So, it's not just a difference between um UK and uh American spellings. And they made fun of the frivolous names for different shades that had been devised by the fashion industry. Um and they cite a um a 19th century book that um straightfacedly listed all of these ridiculous names for colors of the of the moment like stifled sigh and um burnt opera house. And they also laugh about um the the name elephant's breath um but say that nobody no one's ever found a sample of this famous elephant's breath. So everybody derides it as this ridiculous color name but no one's ever been actually actually able to prove what color it is. Um and they also identified the inadequacy of a lot of color terms that were thought to be universal such as nut brown and flesh. They asked you know what sort of nut are you talking about? What color of flesh do you mean? Um there's the you can see the entry for nut brown there at the bottom of the lefthand page and they describe that as completely generic in character. There are various kinds of nuts. Most of them are brown but the brown is a different color in each case. There is no such thing as an average nut. And their work had quite a bit of practical application. It was referred to by the US Department of Agriculture to specify acceptable colors for food stuff such as canned peas and grapefruit juice. Um, peas had to reach a particular shade of green. I forget which one, but one of these squares was like the the threshold of acceptable greenness. Um, so it had a very very practical application. There's a great book about this called Visualizing Taste by Ivano about the history of food coloring. um which is worth a look if you're interested in in that particular um angle. So dictionary of color emerged at a time of great proliferation of color schemes as as mentioned and on the left for instance just to put it in some context here's a scheme to identify the color of flowers that was produced in 1900's Paris and on the right is the UK's Wilson color scheme of the 1930s which borrowed heavily from the Parisian design. uh but interestingly both try to integrate different terms for the colors in different systems to say this is the equivalent across these different systems. So it's more these sorts of um publications were just as much a kind of color thesaurus as a color dictionary. And then in the early 20th century also had the Msel system uh which abandoned poetic and descriptive terms altogether and just used alpha numeric codes to show where a color variant would fit into its 3D model where each iteration of each color can be expressed in terms of three variables. So where it sits on a spectrum of hue, value and chroma. So the example Mel gives in this I think is that a standard vermillion would be R59. So R for red um five for where it appears on the light and dark scale and nine for where it appears in the on the saturation scale. And I often show this to to students who are visiting when we're talking about the three-dimensional qualities of color. And um we talk about how this is more or less the same as the slider bars that they're used to working with in Photoshop um but doing it all on the screen of course. and sometimes color names became almost localized or nationalized. So we have a lot of publications of the British Color Council um including their textile swatch of 1934. You can see a little fragment of it there. Um so the British Color Council was a kind of semiofficial body that tried to regulate the use of color in public buildings and on flags and so on. And this um uh uh standard um issue of color names um contain terms such as post office red and union jack blue. very kind of UK ccentric. Uh and we have a similar publication from around the same same time from the has terms such as eggplant and old glory um that are comparatively meaningless in the UK. So, since I've been touching a little bit on color and commerce, that brings us on to my third and final title, which was a real revelation to me when I was researching my own book. It was just one of those ones I plucked off the shelves and and read from cover to cover and loved. Um, this is Faber Bern selling color to people, which is a classic from 1956. So, Bern was one of the 20th century's great self-styled color gurus. uh he dropped out of university to pursue a career as a color consultant and he had great success advising industry on using color effectively especially during the explosion of commercial color in post-war America um traditionally thought of as a time of optimism and bright pallets and deep pockets for consumer goods. I don't know where this image of him on the left is from. Um, I've seen it a couple of times, but I don't I don't know what the origin of it was, whether it was for a like a profile in a magazine or something, but I really love this this publicity of image of him surrounded by all of his um color charts and and files of information and so on. Um, it's a little bit Norman Rockwell and a little bit Mad Men. Um, he's comes across as sort of both folksy and and businesslike. And you get both of those kind of sides of his personality and his in his writing as well. He always strikes me as this kind of blend of a quite a profound and deep scholar but also a very sharp canny marketer. Um he was really steeped in color history and symbolism. But he also took a hard-nosed approach to color as something that can make people behave in certain ways. And he devised the concept of what he called functional color. uh meaning color in environments that has no aesthetic value but has a purpose to influence human behavior. So uh one thing he did was design national color schemes for hazard signage around heavy equipment using different colors to denote risk of electrocution or burning or other sort of horrible injuries to get people to step away from things that might um might cause them uh to regret it. Uh so one of the ways of course that color could be a powerful force uh to exert on people was as a means to get people to part with their cash. And Bern understood this better than anyone. For example, he showed a meat wholesaler um that they could increase sales by painting the walls of their showrooms a pale green, which made the cuts of meat on display look more appetizing than against white. uh there was a kind of science behind his recommendations, but there was also crucially a lot of hard data. So all of his advice to manufacturers and retailers was informed by long-term studies of sales charts and how sales differed with different colorways on offer and what the kind of demographic was interested in buying. He was a user re researcher before it was common practice and he convened focus groups and he commissioned surveys to inform his findings. So here are a couple of the observations and findings from the book. On the left is the notion that color ranges become narrower and simpler the more mass market the product. High fashion at the top has lots of colors, very eccentric, very unconventional, very flamboyant, but the closer it filters down to the high street, the more the colors become limited and predictable. Um, he thought the public was quite conservative and quite basic in its color tastes, but he thought this was a good thing. Um, he viewed people as sort of unpretentious and pure rather than unsophisticated. He thought there was a kind of purity and sort of honesty and pragmatism to their taste that had to be sort of celebrated but also worked with. Um I love the chart on the right uh which shows that um if you were to launch a product in multiple colorways hardly any of them would sell. um in this particular chart uh I think there's something like 22 different uh colors for the same product and almost all of them uh all almost all of the sales 69% of the sales come from the top five colors and the rest kind of largely languish on the shelves. Hardly anybody buys them. Um, he also found that the the average consumer didn't really like buying a product that was in a color they couldn't quite name. If they thought there was something slightly off about the color or they couldn't quite work out whether it was, you know, blue or green or somewhere in between, that would be very off-putting. People liked sort of very pure, easily definable and recognizable colors. And this book came out during a surge of interest in pop psychology and psychoanalysis and so on. um but also the ascendants of marketing and advertising. Uh it was published just a year before Vance Packard's explosive book, The Hidden Persuaders, which presented advertising as a manipulative, malevolent force, making people buy things they didn't need and planting subliminal messages to control our spending patterns. Colorful packaging was one of the ways to do this. So Bern and Packard both recognized the growing power of advertisers, but also I think the complicity or projection on the part of members of the public who wanted to use color as a form of self-expression, as a marker of aspiration and and status. Again, this is all way ahead of its time and it anticipates the popular science books of the last couple of decades on consumer behavior titles like um nudge and predictably irrational that whole kind of nudge theory of economics. Um Bern was anticipating a great deal of that and Bern's ideas percolated throughout industry and they turned up in lots of places from the academic to the populist. Um, in the collection we've got multiple copies of this lovely little booklet that was created by Bern. Uh, that was a quick guide to your personality type as defined by your favorite color. Uh, and it's often quite unflattering. So, I mean, I my favorite color is yellow. And it says that anybody who likes yellow has a horribly high opinion of themselves. So, I don't know quite what to make of that, but um, it's a little bit like a horoscope and that you can kind of read what you like into it. And you can even see that with the kind of um sort of crystal ball type uh illustration on on the front. Um but crucially of course uh this looks like a fun little booklet, but it's also an advert and it's ultimately trying to sell people a range of paints and it's ends by the punch line is no matter what color you like, we've got a paint to sell you. first and Bernin's work um his his work in the 1950s anticipates one of the most popular works in the color reference library uh which dates actually from the late 60s early '7s. This is the Lucia color test by the Swiss psychologist Max Lucia um who claimed to be able to divine people's personality types and neurosis uh based solely on their color preferences. And each copy came with these eight cards. And the reader was supposed to look at them without thinking about it, pair them off, um, sort of subconsciously pick their two favorite colors, then their second two favorite, then the third two favorite down to their least two favorite, and then you turn the the cards over, they're all numbered. um you work through the kind of mathematical formula in the book and depending on the order in which you're adding them up and what they add up to uh you get this incredibly acute kind of psychological profile. Um uh people laugh about it now. Uh Lucia was quite defensive and said it's definitely not a parlor game. You know, it's serious business. Um but uh it's interesting to me that all the all the colors seem sort of slightly off in that there's kind of not an obvious kind of red. there's a very strange sort of yellow and so on. I think he must have put a lot of thought into um making them deliberately those colors that you can't quite easily name so that you're not just thinking, "Oh, I love red, so I'm going to go straight for red." Because there isn't quite the sort of red you'd expect to see there. Um, but this is as good a title as any um I think to to finish on because all visitors as mentioned seem to love it. And it's one of those titles that really cuts across all the different types of people that I welcome to the to the color reference library who all bring different expectations and understandings of color. Um, people can take the Lucia test seriously or not, but there's something quite um, uh, impressive and profound, I think, about the just the sort of primacy of staring at these colors and, uh, weighing up your feelings about them. That seems to really sort of appeal to everyone, whether they buy into the psychology or not. And like a lot of the titles in the color reference library, it's about the experience of color as much as the idea of it. So, so many of the books in the color reference library speak to that universality of color as something we all have a relationship with and that always has the capacity to surprise and delight and maybe even subvert. And really, these are the qualities that we find across all the shelves, all the 2,00 books. They pull all the centuries and subjects together. They bind all the users and all their questions. They let our readers immerse themselves and find new ways of thinking about a subject as old as our existence and as wide as our lives. Thank you very much. Thank you very much indeed, Neil. That was uh great. Oh, we're seeing some applause applause reactions there all over the place. That that's really great. Um, so we haven't had any questions in the chat yet, but please, if anyone wants to ask a question, maybe just initially put it into the chat. Um, I just had one question. Are have you been active in digitizing any of the older volumes in your library, especially ones that might be unique to your library? >> Yeah, that's that's a great question. The answer is yes. So, we were lucky enough to get some funding uh back in 2018 to buy some pretty decent book scanners and one that uses like two digital SLR cameras and so on. Um, and I I work with a very good um like project officer when we got that funding who photographed quite a lot of color plates in the books. And that was the the initial um uh driver for that was to to illustrate my own book because we wanted to be able to license images that we' we'd taken to the publisher. Um uh and then also just to have them um for anybody else who who who wanted to use them. Um, so we've we've built up quite a a sort of a digital library, not yet available to the public, though we're working on that, but we do have photos of a lot of the key color plates from a lot of the classic work. So the a lot of the ones I mentioned like Mary Garroll and so on. Um, obviously color management is a is a huge issue uh with trying to adequately capture um uh hand colored plates. Uh, so that's something we've had to be very mindful of and that's posed certain challenges. Um, the Merry Gart side is is a good one if you know that it has all these different colored blotss of kind of light and shade and I know people who've been to see it in different libraries around the world because no two copies are alike because they're all kind of handc colored and they're all kind of different manifestations. Uh so um you know we're really keen to to share that sort of that the images of the of the the um uh the books that we have. Um we're not you know possessive about them and we we want we want to to share those images. Um when I started writing my my book it was lockdown so I couldn't actually get to my own library my I couldn't get to the color reference library to actually look at a lot of the books. So we ended up having to do uh having to look at a lot of them on the internet archives uh digitized versions that had come from you know a lot of libraries in the US and elsewhere and that was so valuable to have that resource but I was also aware that when I did get back in and looked at our original physical copies that something very different takes place when you're looking at kind of hand handc colored works from the 18th and 19th centuries. So I'm aware that digitization can't completely replace that experience. >> Sure. Sure. Um that uh that would be the the history of color, a universe of chromic phenomena would be the book you're mentioning there. Uh Neil, >> that that would be the one. >> Excellent. Excellent. Um I think there a few Oh, a few things have come in the chat. I don't know. Um would you like me to read them or would you like uh There's one here. Um, uh, let's see. Can we check what books are in the library? Um, >> is the catalog online? >> We can. >> Mhm. >> Yes, the catalog is online. Um, so if you go to library.rca.ac.uk, UK. That is our kind of discovery platform. And it's quite difficult to narrow your search down to the color reference library. But the way to do it is to do a blank search. Just search for nothing. And you'll get something like 400,000 results. And what you then need to do is filter your results. So there's there's um an option in the left hand side of the screen called library location. And you go down to that bit and you tick the box that says color reference library. Suddenly your 400,000 titles will turn into something like 2,000 titles and that will show you just what is in the color reference library. So >> that's a great tip. Thank you. And um there's a question here. Um who is the library open to? Can we book in to use it? >> Yes, absolutely you can. Um there's only me and a part-time member of staff working on all the RCA's special collection. So we are quite busy, but we do warmly welcome external researchers. Um, you just the best thing to do is to email um, uh, special-colctions rca.ac.uk. Uh, and that will come to either me or my colleague Natasha and one of us will will get back to you. Um, one of the things I say is you don't have to know exactly what you want to see before you come. Uh, because we do allow browsing on the shelves. You can make a selection and then then we'll bring the books into the reading room. I tried to make it so that you don't just have to pick things from the library catalog because it has so many weird and wonderful things in it that actually um you know you don't know what you're going to find and you you might think that you're interested in something and then you find something completely different on the shelves. That kind of serendipitous browsing is something we we really um want to support. >> Fantastic. Great. Uh there's a question just came in. Has there been any other theorist as influential on artists as Chavel? Oh, I don't know. Uh, I mean, there's certainly been a few like, um, Ogden Rude uh, for instance. Yes. >> Um, uh, I'm sure there's a few. Uh, Chro was an interesting one because of, well, I remember John Gage, the the late great John Gage, uh, brilliant historian of color and art, came to give a talk at the RCA years ago, soon after I I started in conjunction with the the color group of of Great Britain, and he said, "Oh, so many artists just are not interested in color theory and historically just haven't embraced color theory." And I find it a lot actually uh in working with artists in the color reference library. Quite often the last thing they want to know is the science behind it and they feel that that completely kind of neutralizes and kills off the creative process. So it's often it's often quite difficult to tease out how much um the the kind of scientific writing uh of um of you know scholars explaining color theory and explaining perception and contrast and so on how much that actually filters down. There are obvious people like Bridget Riley let's say who's completely embraced a lot of theories and really worked with it to get her optical illusions. Obviously David Bachelor is incredibly steeped in in color theory. Um but it's actually um tracing a line between the original publication and the influence in art is is quite is quite challenging certainly up there is massively influential and still is. >> Yeah sure. Um >> uh can you give other examples of how artists use your collection for inspiration? Maybe you want to give one more or >> I'm just trying to think whether I should name names. Uh there there so there were there's a very well-known well pretty well-known artist who worked like overwhelmingly with color who came to try to do essentially a new version of dictionary of color uh and just to try to itemize every color uh ever and to to kind of make a make a book out of it and found as so many people did that the scale of it defeated them. But they did have a lovely time looking at all of the all of the um all of the samples. Um one of the books I put up was by um Marian Duchars who's a wonderful um RCA alumna uh and um uh illustrator and designed loads of um book jackets and things very well known here. And she did a wonderful book about um about color uh that uh I included a slide of. Um, there are lots really, but it's actually I tend not to keep tabs on them too much. I kind of deal with them when they come in and then they go away and I don't always get to see what gets created as a result. One of the nice things about having our annual show, um, our degree show when students kind of show off everything that they've done at their time at the RCA is that I'll suddenly think, "Oh, yes, I remember that person. They came to see me last October." And you can see how the books that they've absorbed, how it's kind of percolated into their work. And it's not not not um just fine artists by any stretch. There's a lot of ceramicists who use the collection, for instance, and and use that to inspire three-dimensional color. Threedimensional color is a question I get quite a lot because a lot of the color theory that's in the books is very much kind of flat and static and side by side on the page. Um, but we have a lot of people working in three dimensions, of course. >> Oh, great. They're coming in, but I think we might have to wrap it up soon. There's one I missed a little bit earlier on from Ruth. That's looks interesting. Has neuroscience opened up new ideas about the experience of color and does the library have any books on this? >> That is a good question. And I would say that's possibly one of the shortcomings of the color reference library that it is still even though we buy new books about color it is still quite um historical and it's quite slowm moving um and that we don't have very much about cutting edge technology and cutting edge um findings. They take quite a while to percolate down into books. Um so when people ask me questions like that uh I will often sort of refer them to the online journals and things that the college subscribes to or they might just have to wait um or they might have to you know write the write the research themselves because we don't we don't have um we don't have everything. Um, but yeah, in terms of sort of color perception, there's certainly quite a lot about it in the in the color reference library, but it tends to be probably the among the least popular titles again because people want that kind of experiential uh relationship with color, the sort of users we attract. Anyway, if we were at Imperial College, there's the the um like science and technology university down the road from us, I think we'd have a very different user base, but we have a user base that wants kind of um emotional re relationship with color, uh practical, immersive, experiential, that sort of thing rather than the neuroscience side of things. >> Thank you.
